coredata.dc:title: Comprehensive investigation of Prunus armeniaca for natural green synthesis of carbon quantum dots; Applications as fluorescent nano-probes for ramipril
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Salman, Baher I.
Batakoushy, Hany A.
Saraya, Roshdy E.
Hassan, Ahmed I.
Al-Harrasi, Ahmed
Ibrahim, Adel Ehab
coredata.dc:description: The use of natural precursors for green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is attracting significant attention. However, concerns regarding the characters and performance of CQDs derived from the same natural resource can be raised. In the proposed research, Prunus armeniaca (apricots) juice was utilized as a natural precursor for CQDs through a single-step microwave-assisted method at 700 W for 5 min. The obtained CQDs were characterized and then assessed against those few that had been reported from apricots from different geographical and botanical origins. The developed CQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescent characters with red-shift emission (430–510 nm). The greener and cost-effective synthesis pathway had a higher quantum yield (37.1 %), with nano-sized CQDs (≈2.8 nm), and chemical composition of C (44.79 %), O (38.29 %), and N (16.92 %). The developed nanoprobes were then investigated for determination of ramipril (RAL) antihypertensive drug for the first time using this tool. They were found selective and sensitive for RAL determination in bulk powders and biological plasma within the range of 2.0–100.0 ng mL−1, where the limits of detection and quantification were 0.58 and 1.76 ng mL−1. The incorporation of RAL led to a notable decrease in the luminescence response of the synthesized green and stable CQDs at 505 nm (excitation 460 nm). Furthermore, the storage stability (in refrigerator) and reusability of the proposed CQDs were effectively confirmed for RAL analysis for 10 weeks. The proposed tool was finally compared to some other reported techniques and was found to be more efficient and cost-effective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Apricots
Green natural precursors
Carbon dots
Fluorescent nanoprobes
Ramipril determination

Reaction mechanism of the proposed method

Validation of the N@CQDs proposed approach in RAL assessment
Applications of the developed N@CQDs and investigating their reusability
Evaluation of the developed N@CQDs to previously synthesized CQDs from apricots as green resources
Assessing the proposed tool for RAL determination relative to some reported tools
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Informed consent statement
Funds
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of RAL (a), together with UV spectrum (b), excitation dependent emission spectra (c), and FTIR spectrum (d) for the green synthesized N@CQDs.
TEM screening (a and b), DLS analysis (c) and Raman spectroscopy (d) for the developed N@CQDs.
EDX spectrum for the naturally developed N@CQDs.
Reaction of the proposed N@CQDs with different concentrations of RAL (a) and the effect of pH and volume of buffer for their reaction with 50 ng mL−1 of RAL (b).
Quantification parameters for analysis of RAL using the green N@CQDs approach.
Accuracy and precision studies for assay of RAL using the proposed approach.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of three determinations; RSD: Relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Comprehensive investigation of Prunus armeniaca for natural green synthesis of carbon quantum dots; Applications as fluorescent nano-probes for ramipril

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Natural and Medical Sciences Research Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of natural precursors for green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is attracting significant attention. However, concerns regarding the characters and performance of CQDs derived from the same natural resource can be raised. In the proposed research, Prunus armeniaca (apricots) juice was utilized as a natural precursor for CQDs through a single-step microwave-assisted method at 700 W for 5 min. The obtained CQDs were characterized and then assessed against those few that had been reported from apricots from different geographical and botanical origins. The developed CQDs showed excitation-dependent photoluminescent characters with red-shift emission (430–510 nm). The greener and cost-effective synthesis pathway had a higher quantum yield (37.1 %), with nano-sized CQDs (≈2.8 nm), and chemical composition of C (44.79 %), O (38.29 %), and N (16.92 %). The developed nanoprobes were then investigated for determination of ramipril (RAL) antihypertensive drug for the first time using this tool. They were found selective and sensitive for RAL determination in bulk powders and biological plasma within the range of 2.0–100.0 ng mL−1, where the limits of detection and quantification were 0.58 and 1.76 ng mL−1. The incorporation of RAL led to a notable decrease in the luminescence response of the synthesized green and stable CQDs at 505 nm (excitation 460 nm). Furthermore, the storage stability (in refrigerator) and reusability of the proposed CQDs were effectively confirmed for RAL analysis for 10 weeks. The proposed tool was finally compared to some other reported techniques and was found to be more efficient and cost-effective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The emergence and evolution of carbon-based nanomaterials have been notably blooming during the past 2 decades. Such carbon-based nanomaterials exhibited numerous applicability and are being favored in different scientific areas, owing to their sustainability, biocompatibility and low ecological fingerprint. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a typical class of such materials with unique optical and electronic properties [1]. These nano-probes exhibited distinctive characteristics, including high solubility in aqueous environments, together with remarkable stability. CQDs are strongly fluorescent tunable probes that can be designed to interact with target substrates or analytes, resulting in variations in the fluorescent output. The synthesis of such zero-dimensional C-based nanomaterials have been investigated from different economic, sustainable and renewable resources. Fruits are one of the best resources for synthesis of CQDs, being rich in different functional groups trapped in form of proteins, carbohydrates, vitamins and other bioactive substrates [2].

Meanwhile, several methods were introduced for the synthesis of CQDs using bottom-up technique (Hydrothermal, micro-wave assisted, solvothermal, and electrochemical) or top-down technique (Laser ablation, arc discharge, or high energy ball milling). The synthetic procedure has a high influence on the performance and characteristics of the developed QDs [3]. It can affect their size and composition, their quantum yield, stability and also fluorescent properties [4,5]. For instance, the slight variation in synthesis media introduced by Li et al. [6] on o-phenylenediamine gave results to 2 different yellow and orange fluorescence CQDs, with different average particle sizes and emission maxima. The ultrafast microwave hydrothermal method is an innovative approach for synthesizing CQDs with unique optical and electronic properties, combining speed and efficiency with potential for various applications in technology and medicine [7,8]. Meanwhile, such technique is suitable to be utilized with several natural precursors like polysaccharides [9,10], l-cystine [11], watercress [12], or other organic materials that can decompose to form carbon nanoparticles.

Prunus armeniaca (Apricot) fruits are widely distributed globally, especially within the mediterranean region, with rich phenolic and carotenoids contents [13]. Few papers have been reported recently on the use of apricot fruits as a natural resource for synthesis of CQDs for different applications [14–19]. Mujahid used the apricot peel [15], and Patil et al. used the grated fruit [16] as resource for their CQDs for sensing and quantification of the inorganic salts of Cd(II) and Cr(IV), respectively. Xu et al. developed CQDs from the apricot shell waste which were used as nano-probes for ascorbic acid [14]. On the other side, apricot leaves were also used to develop CQDs that were used for the inorganic sensing of Cu(II) [17]. Other applications of Nanohybrid CQDs derived from Prunus armeniaca (Apricot) leaf waste and unripe fruit extract resources were utilized recently as environmentally friendly remediations for the removal of toxic Congo-red dye [18] and acid-orange 7 dye [19]. The aforementioned CQDs were processed from apricot using hydrothermal techniques using autoclave [14–17] or heating [19], which were then well-characterized for their purpose.

Ramipril (RAL, Fig. 1 a) is an FDA approved angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor drug for in the management of hypertension and heart failure. ACE-inhibitors blocks the action of the enzyme that converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II, a substance that narrows blood vessels and increases blood pressure. The mechanism of action involves the dilation of blood vessels, thereby facilitating the heart's ability to circulate blood throughout the body. RAL is often prescribed following a myocardial infarction, to help reducing the risk of subsequent strokes, heart attacks, and renal complications. Furthermore, it enhances survival rates in patients with heart failure or those recovering from a heart attack [20,21]. Several methods were reported for the determination of RAL in different matrices including HPLC [22,23], electrochemical [24,25], UV- and fluorescent spectrometry [26,27]. Up to our knowledge, no method has been reported yet to utilize CQDs for determination of RAL.

The main aim of the proposed study is to develop a new feasible and easy microwave-assisted synthetic pathway for engineering novel CQDs utilizing apricots as a green resource. The proposed study emphasizes the properties of the developed nitrogen doped CQDs (N@CQDs), and their quantum yield (QY). These properties were compared to those CQDs that have been recently reported utilizing the same natural resource, apricots, for different applications. Then, the developed N@CQDs were investigated as nano-biosensors for determination of RAL in different matrices and then evaluated against reported methodologies in terms of performance and ecological footprint. The ecological impact of the whole procedure was assessed using different greenness metrics and the approach's potential for enhancing the detection and quantification of RAL in both biological and tablet formulations was challenged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Standards and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: RAL pure analytical standard (99.80 %) was obtained from Adwia Pharmaceuticals Company (Cairo, Egypt). Prunus armeniaca (Apricot) fruits were acquired from the local Egyptian market (Harvest June 2024). RAL stock standard was prepared in methanol at a concentration (100 μg mL−1). Tritace® 10 mg tablets were obtained from local Egyptian pharmacies, manufactured by Sanofi (Cairo, Egypt). Blank plasma samples were purchased from the Egyptian Blood Bank.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instruments
ce:section.ce:para: A Medfuture microwave digester was used for the synthesis of the green CQDs, model MFMI-100A (Jinan, China). pH adjustments were performed using Metrohm 780 m (Herisau, Switzerland). Fluorescence spectrometry experiments were performed on Scinco FluoroMate FS-2 fluorescence spectrometer, manufactured by Scinco (Seoul, Korea). FluoroMate FS-2 was equipped with xenon (150W) light source ozone-free, and quartz sample cells 1 cm depth. TEM images were captured using a JEM-100CXII high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) produced by JEOL in Tokyo, Japan. The powder X-ray diffraction was conducted using a Philips X-ray diffractometer (model PW3040/00) obtained from the Philips Innovation Lab in Eindhoven, Netherlands. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was done on InfraLUM FT-08 FTIR produced by Lumex instruments, headquartered in Mission, Canada. The ZEN 3600 Zetasizer employing dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques and produced by Malvern Instruments was utilized for particle size analysis, which was calibrated within size range of 0.3–10,000 nm, and the manufacturing company was based in Malvern, UK. The elemental analysis was carried out using Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometer (EDX) JEOL JSM-5400 LV instrument (Tokyo, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed for elemental analysis using ESCALAB250 spectrometer from Thermo-Fisher Scientific (Massachusetts, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the developed green N@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The production of N@CQDs was accomplished using a simple microwave assisted technique, as detailed by Salman et al. [28]. Briefly, the apricot fruit was processed in a mixer to make juice after removing the pit. A 50 mL solution of fruit juice was placed in a conical flask and subjected to microwave radiation at 700 W for 5 min, resulting in a brown solution. The solution was sonicated for 20 min and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Centrifugation helps the solution to separate larger aggregates from smaller, more uniform dots to isolate quantum dots of a specific size. Finally, the solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm cellulose membrane. The resultant solution was then utilized for further analysis and characterization. For further purification, the lyophilization technique was utilized when necessary.

The key aspects for characterization of the developed N@CDQs includes size and shaper determination which were done using TEM imaging and DLS. Surface chemistry was assessed using FTIR technique. The optical properties of the developed CQDS were assessed using UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) and powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) spectra were utilized to evaluate the composition and structure of the developed N@CDQs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the novel green N@CQDs in RAL analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The developed N@CQDs were employed to assess their sensitivity and responsiveness to RAL in various matrices. The interaction response between the N@CQDs and RAL was evaluated at different concentrations to derive their potential as efficient probes for detecting and quantifying this important pharmaceutical compound. For the preparation of the calibration graph, a 1.0 mL solution of N@CQDs dispersion (at concentration of 0.25 mg mL−1), was mixed with 1.5 mL of Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer at pH 7.4. Subsequently, 1.0 mL of RAL standard solution was added within concentration range of 1.0–100.0 ng mL−1. The mixture was thoroughly mixed, and the volume was adjusted to 10 mL mark using ultra-pure water. After a 12-min incubation period, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 505 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 460 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N@CQDs to pharmaceutical dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: Ten tablets of Tritace® (10 mg per tablet) were accurately weighed. Each tablet was mechanically crushed to produce a fine powder, which represent 10 mg of RAL, then was dissolved in 50 mL of methanol through sonication for 5 min. The resulting solution was then diluted to a total volume of 100 mL with additional methanol. Afterward, filtration was conducted, and the supernatant was collected for subsequent analysis following the procedure stated in subsection 2.4. The content uniformity was tested for Tritace® 10 mg lot according to procedures listed in the united states pharmacopoeia for tablet formulations [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of RAL in human plasma using the proposed tool
ce:section.ce:para: Human blank plasma samples were preserved at a temperature of −20 °C before analysis. A combination of 0.5 mL of blank plasma was spiked with different concentrations of RAL using vortex mixer. Subsequently, protein precipitation was done by adding 0.5 mL of methanol and vortex mixed once again for 5 min. The resultant blend was competed to a final volume of 10 mL using water and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min. Ultimately, 1.0 mL of the supernatant solution was analyzed for RAL following the procedure stated in subsection 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of the developed N@CQDs and stability study of RAL in plasma using the fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the reusability of N@CQDs, a recycling test was performed, involving the quantification of RAL (90 ng mL−1) across ten iterations over a ten-weeks period. At the end of each cycle, the quantum dots were isolated using centrifugation, carefully washed with ultra-pure water and ethanol, and dried for 30 min at 50 °C to confirm their reusability. Subsequently, the quantum dots were kept at 4 °C for later use.

The stability of RAL in human plasma was studied using the N@CQDs. RAL was assessed at 3 quality control levels: low, medium and high (LQC, MQC and HQC, respectively) at concentrations 40.0, 60.0 and 90.0 ng mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Selection of apricots as natural green resource
ce:section.ce:para: C-based nanomaterials have been enriched with the developments in QDs as novel biosensors during the past 2 decades. The recent sustainability trends aim at using renewable and green resources in the manufacture and application of these novel nanomaterials. Fruits are abundant, biodegradable, and contain various biomolecules, that can serve as reducing and stabilizing agents in the synthesis of quantum dots. Among these fruits, apricots (Prunus armeniaca) were classified among the top dietary sources of polyphenols and natural functional food. Apricot has shown varying phenolic contents, primarily based on the geographical regions of cultivation as well as environmental conditions. These phytochemical constituents in Prunus armeniaca are varied also according to their plant parts, ripening state, or when being circulated from waste [30,31]. Therefore, apricots have been recently presented as a promising avenue for green synthesis of CQDs. As mentioned earlier, few research publications have been reported using apricot as renewable biodegradable resource which was used for different applications [14–19]. These research studies were conducted in different geographical regions, using different apricot cultivars, engineered via different synthetic pathways and used for different applications. Therefore, it was chosen to comprehensively explore its new possibilities in nanotechnology and its applications across various fields, through comparing the characters and applicability of CQDs as proposed here relative to recent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic features of the developed N@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: After micro-wave assisted synthesis of N@CQDs from apricots juice, their optical properties were examined using UV-spectrum and fluorescence spectroscopy. N@CQDs showed two significant absorption maxima at 285 nm and 330 nm, as shown in Fig. 1b. These absorption peaks are indicative of the C-skeleton structure and the functional groups electronic transitions [28]. The photoluminescent spectra were analyzed by scanning fluorescence spectroscopy. The emission spectra showed a red shift when excitation wavelength was increased, transitioning between 430 nm and 510 nm. The relative fluorescent intensity (RFI) showed a decrease accompanying the observed red-shift, which confirms the excitation-dependent nature of the emission from the carbon dots (Fig. 1c) [32].

Furthermore, the surface morphology of the N@CQDs was performed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). The FTIR spectroscopy curve elucidates the diagnostic peaks associated with the (-NH, –OH) –CH groups, observed at 3430 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 1640 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 1210 cm−1 are attributed to the –CO, –CN and -C-O-C groups. Meanwhile, performing IR spectroscopy for the source material before synthesizing quantum dots is beneficial to identify the functional groups and chemical bonds in the source material. The IR spectrum of the natural resource of the developed CQDs was obtained as presented in supplementary materials (SM; Fig. S1) which indicated the presence of functional groups which are essential for synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots.

In addition, to assess the electrical charges on the surface of N@CQDs and their colloidal stability, a zeta potential analysis was performed. The measured zeta potential values for N@CQDs are presented in (SM Fig. S2). The zeta potential was found to be −12.64 mV, suggesting the existence of negatively charged carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) functional groups on the surface.

The QY of the green synthesized CQDs was performed using a comparative single point method in comparison to reference rhodamine B solution [28]. The following formula was used where Qst is the fluorescent QY of the standard (QY = 0.31), η represents the refractive index of water as solvent, and I is the intensity of fluorescence [28]. The QY of the newly developed N@CQDs was calculated to be 37.17 %.
Q X
=
Q
s t
.
I X
I
s t
.
A
s t
A X
.
η 2
η 2

The particle size of the developed nano-biosensors is an important critical factor that significantly influences their properties and applications [33]. The size of particles was measured using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM; Fig. 2 a and b) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2c). TEM screening showed an average particle size of 2.6 ± 0.27 nm with lattice spacing 0.21 nm. Additionally, the particle size distribution using Zetasizer of N@CQDs was evaluated through DLS, yielding a measurement of 2.8 ± 0.29 nm as presented in Fig. 2c, with a mono-dispersity value of 0.14. Furthermore, Raman spectrometer analysis as a vibrational molecular spectroscopy has also substantial role for investigating the molecular structure attributes of N@CQDs and providing insights into the degree of graphitization and the presence of defects [34,35]. Fig. 2d displays the Raman spectrum, which features two prominent peaks at 1347 and 1587 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of N@CQDs [35].

An extended elemental analysis (EDX) was carried out after lyophilization the sample for the detection of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements, as depicted in Fig. 3 . The analysis revealed that the composition of the carbon dots is primarily C (44.79 %), O (38.29 %), and N (16.92 %). The XPS analysis clarifies the elemental structure and bonding characteristics of carbon dots. The survey spectrum indicates the presence of three main elements, characterized by their specific binding energies: carbon (287.16 eV), nitrogen (403.24 eV), and oxygen (535.19 eV), which confirms the successful incorporation of these elements into the carbon dot architecture (SM Fig. S3a). C1s spectra (SM Fig. S3b) identifies four distinct peaks; sp2 carbon and C–C bonds at 284.98 eV, C–N/C–O bonds at 285.87 eV, CO groups at 287.31 eV, and (O–CO) at 289.04 eV. This suggests a complex carbon architecture with a variety of functional groups. The O1s spectrum reveals two distinct oxygen environments: one peak at 531.84 eV associated with CO bonds and another at 533.14 eV linked to C–O bonds (Fig. S3c). N1s (SM Fig. S3d) provides two peaks at 399.87 eV and 401.57 eV for C–N–C and N–H groups, respectively.

The PXRD analysis of N@CQDs reveals a prominent diffraction peak at approximately 26.40°, corresponding to the [002] pattern (SM Fig. S4). This observation suggests that the synthesized N@CQDs possess an amorphous structure, attributed to the densely packed carbon atoms with alkyl branches and a significant presence of oxygen-containing species on their surface [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed N@CQDs were assessed for their interaction with RAL. As depicted in Fig. 4 a–N@CQDs exhibited a gradual decline in their fluorescence intensity following the addition of different concentrations of RAL. This interaction plays a crucial role in quenching the fluorescence of N@CQDs. Such quenching could be facilitated by multiple mechanisms, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer, the internal filtering effect, and the dynamic/static quenching [37,38]. Studying the absorption spectrum of RAL showed that it has an absorption maximum at 210 nm, which does not correspond with the fluorescence emission of N@CQDs at 505 nm (SM Fig. S5). This in turn excludes the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism in quenching. As shown (SM Fig. S5), there is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of RAL and the N@CQDs emission spectrum at 505 nm, especially in relation to the internal filter effect [37,38].

To distinguish the dynamic from static quenching, Stern-Volmer graph was plotted, and its equation was employed (SM Fig. S6). The equation is represented as F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q], where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, which provides information about the efficiency of the quenching process. As illustrated in SM Fig. S6, the linearity of the Stern-Volmer plot indicates the presence of a dynamic quenching mechanism. The interaction between RAL and excited N@QDs in such dynamic quenching facilitates electron and the energy transfer, which contributes to the observed quenching of the quantum dot luminescence [39]. To further confirm the dynamic quenching mechanism, experiments were conducted on the RAL/N@CQDs interactions at varying temperatures. The findings demonstrated that with an increase in temperature, the Stern-Volmer constant also rose, supporting the dynamic quenching hypothesis (SM Fig. S6). M oreover, the presence of various functional groups on the surface of the quantum dots encourages the formation of hydrogen bonds and enhances electrostatic interactions between N@CQDs and RAL [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the proposed N@CQDs approach for RAL assay
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the interaction between RAL and the developed N@CQDs is governed by several factors affecting. These factors were studied carefully to boost the reactivity and hence the sensitivity of the developed N@CQDs against minute concentration of the quencher. The key variables included the concentration of N@CQDs, their time of interaction with RAL, and the volume used of the buffer and its pH.

Fig. 4b shows the effect of changing buffer pH and its used volume on the intensity of fluorescence obtained during the interaction of fluorescent N@CQDs and RAL. The buffer's pH was analyzed within the range of 6.2–8.4, and its added volume between 0.5 and 2.75 mL. The highest fluorescent signal was achieved at pH 7.4 and using 1.5 ± 0.25 mL of BR buffer.

The impact of fluorescent N@CQDs concentration on its quenching interaction with RAL. Various concentrations of N@CQDs, specifically between 0.2 and 0.3 mg mL−1, were evaluated. The results (SM Fig. S7) indicated that at 0.25 mg mL−1 of the developed N@CQDs, the highest RFI was obtained.

Furthermore, the microwave parameters for synthesizing the green N@CQDs were carefully optimized. The synthesis duration was tested across a range of time intervals from 1 to 10 min, with the highest QY occurring at 5 min under a power setting of 700W (SM Fig. S8). This study focused on the effects of interaction time, evaluated over a maximum duration of 30 min. The results demonstrated that the optimal interaction time was 12 min, with any additional time proving unproductive and not affecting the RFI (refer to SM Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the N@CQDs proposed approach in RAL assessment
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed quantum dot approach for assessing the interaction between N@CQDs and RAL was validated in line with ICH guidelines and bioanalytically confirmed according to the United States Food and Drug Administration standards [40,41].

As confirmed (Fig. 4a), the gradual addition of RAL to the quantum dots causes quenching effect measured at 505 nm (excitation at 460 nm). Firstly, a calibration curve was established (SM Fig. S9) by plotting the RFI against various concentrations added of RAL, employing 1.0 mL of the prepared 0.25 mg mL−1 N@CQDs and utilizing the Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q], where F0 is the fluorecent intensity of N@CQDs in the absence of RAL, and F in its presence. The Stern-Volmer constant, Ksv, serves to measure the quenching process, while the concentration of the quencher, [Q], plays a significant role in this evaluation. The calibration range was established between 2.0 and 100.0 ng mL−1 of RAL detailed in Table 1
. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were derived from the slope and standard deviation (σ) of the calibration curve. LOQ was calculated using formula (10 σ/slope), while LOD was determined as (3.3 σ/slope). The resulting LOD of 0.58 ng mL−1 and LOQ of 1.76 ng mL−1, as indicated in Table 1, underscore the high sensitivity of the proposed analytical technique.

Selectivity and specificity are other important parameters in the method validation. Therefore, the study involved evaluating the interference from some different sources that could be found and might compromise the selectivity of the N@CQDs tool for RAL assessment. Interferences including sodium, potassium, cobalt, nickel, zinc, calcium, ferric, talc, starch, and barium (0.5 μg mL−1) were tested to assess the selectivity of the quantum dots approach in various matrices (SM Fig. S10). As shown, the RFI was only affected by the addition/inclusion of RAL in its solution.

To evaluate the reliability of the N@CQDs method, five distinct concentrations (5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 70.0, and 90.0 ng mL−1) of RAL were prepared in solution and analyzed. The percentage recovery results ranged from 99.9 to 101.4 %, confirming the high level of accuracy associated with the N@CQDs technique (Table 2 ). Then, the precision was assessed at 2 levels, repeatability and intermediate precision (intra-day and inter-day variations). This assessment involved measuring three different RAL concentrations at 20.0, 40.0, and 80.0 ng mL−1, with three measurements taken on the same day and across three separate days. The findings indicated the method's excellent precision (Table 2).

Another bio-analytical validation was investigated in human plasma to calibrate the presence of interferences at three different concentrations (30.0, 60.0, and 90.0 ng mL−1) of RAL spiked in blank human plasma. According to Table 3 , the found recovery percentages and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were found in a row with theoretical values. Therefore, the study further evaluated the stability of RAL in human plasma at three quality control levels: low (LQC 40.0 ng mL−1), medium (MQC 60.0 ng mL−1), and high (HQC 90.0 ng mL−1). The recovery percentages, as detailed in (SM Table S11), ranged between 96.18 % and 97.92 %, indicating that RAL exhibits excellent stability in human plasma.

In addition, the robustness of N@CQDs approach for RAL determination was assessed against minor changes in methodological parameters, including N@CQDs concentration, buffer volume, pH, and interaction time. It was observed that these adjustments did not significantly alter the recovery results for RAL determination (SM Table S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the developed N@CQDs and investigating their reusability
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the applicability of the proposed nanoprobes, RAL was assessed in different matrices. RAL was assessed in marketed pharmaceutical dosage forms and introduced at different concentrations into plasma samples devoid of any medications. Subsequently, N@CQDs were employed under the recommended analytical conditions to determine the RAL concentration. The results, presented in Table 4 , indicate notable recovery rates for RAL analyzed from spiked human plasma using the proposed method.

The proposed technique successfully determined the RAL content in Tritace® 10 mg tablets, achieving a good percentage recovery (Table 4). These results underscore the method's reliability and consistency. Content uniformity testing plays a crucial role in the quality control of pharmaceutical tablet dosage forms, ensuring that each tablet's drug content remains within a defined range around the labeled amount. This process is essential for maintaining consistency in dosing across units. Although content uniformity testing can be intricate and time-consuming, the N@CQDs method offered a highly efficient and cost-effective solution. The uniformity test adhered to the USP guideline protocol, and the acceptance value (AV) was calculated as SD (standard deviation) x 2.4. The resulting AV was below the maximum allowable acceptance value (L1), confirming the consistent production of RAL (Table 4).

The results obtained for the analysis of RAL in Tritace® 10 mg tablets were compared to those results obtained for RAL determination using the HPLC technique presented by a previously reported method [23]. The assay results were statistically compared (n = 6) using the t-test and F-value at 95 % confidence interval. The average percentage of RAL was found to be 100.78 ± 0.64 using the developed CQDs, while the average results were 101.01 ± 0.43 (Recovery%±standard deviation). The calculated t-test was 0.1 (2.23) and the F-value was 2.4 (5.05) indicating insignificant differences, while being in line with the manufacturer labeled claims.

The assessment of the reusability of quantum dots involves evaluating their performance over multiple cycles of use. This comprehensive evaluation helps establish the sustainability and efficiency of quantum dots in practical applications. SM Fig. S13 presents the assessment of RAL (90.0 ng mL−1) performed over ten iterations in a 10-week period to determine the reusability of carbon dots. The findings revealed that the RFI system maintained consistent performance during the recycling analysis, highlighting the remarkable reusability of carbon dots across ten cycles. Consequently, N@CQDs show promising potential for extended use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the developed N@CQDs to previously synthesized CQDs from apricots as green resources
ce:section.ce:para: After being discovered in 2004, CQDs have been evolving for wide applications. The research related to them is vast including their resource materials, preparation methods, and the effect of such on the characters of the synthesized CQDs and their applications. Meanwhile, the concept of green chemistry offers a forward-thinking methodology for the formulation and synthesis of chemicals, focusing on mitigating their detrimental effects on the environment. For instance, the green aspects related to CQDs include the use of biocompatible resources, and the application of greener synthetic pathway that is solvent-free, energy efficient and time-effective [42,43]. Synthesis of CQDs involve 2 main approaches, the bottom-up and the top-down techniques. The top-down approach involves the breakdown of large carbon sources into CQDs using difficult reactions such as chemical oxidation, laser ablation, discharge or electrochemical for prolonged reaction time. Meanwhile, the bottom-up techniques synthesize CQDs from smaller carbon structures via hydrothermal, ultrasonication or microwave assisted tools. Therefore, the top-down approach is less favorable energy consuming and less green compared to the bottom-up one [44].

The use of natural precursors for CQDs is being currently widely investigated for being sustainable, inexpensive and widely available. Plant derived precursors are very promising candidates, being very abundant and carbon-rich (45–50 %) [45]. Apricots have presented a novel avenue for sustainable material production; however, further research is needed to optimize the synthesis process and explore the properties of the resulting CQDs. As mentioned earlier, up to our knowledge, CQDs have been synthesized from apricots natural resources in only 6 reported research so far [14–19]. Table 5 details the proposed CQDs in comparison to some previously reported [14–18]. The CQDs developed by Khan et al. [19] were excluded from this comparison since they were multi-component nanohybrid composites, that were developed in multiple steps for aqueous remediation of acid orange dye 7. As detailed, CQDs were synthesized from different apricots parts, including juice, waste shell, peel, grated fruits or leaves. All previously reported research [14–18] utilized hydrothermal tools for synthesis which required long reaction time (180–300 min) compared to the proposed microwave-assisted tool (5 min). The employed heating required higher energy consumption relative to the proposed technique. Compared to the hydrothermal approach, microwave-assisted synthesis has always been recognized as greener, faster and comprise great economical potentials [45].

In the meantime, the QY of our proposed study was the best comprising 37.12 %, where the nearest QY was obtained by He et al. [17] from apricots leaves biomass after 12 h hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C. It's worth mentioning that Xu et al. [14] reported 3.84 % QY, where the authors claimed that it was higher than those previously reported from biomass-waste resources.

Concerning the characters of the developed CQDS, their nano-sizes were comparable (Table 5). However, the proposed CQDs have the best red-shift emitting the light at longer wavelengths (lower energy) compared to previously produced CQDs [14–18]. Red-shifted emissions have the advantage of reducing interferences, thus enhancing the selectivity of the proposed CQDs. This red shift enables a wider gap between the excitation and emission spectra (Stokes shift), reducing interference from crosstalk and improving the clarity and resolution of fluorescence imaging. Lower wavelength shifts (Blue/Green Emission) are more prone to autofluorescence and scattering [46]. The composition of the developed CQDs were almost the same especially with those developed by Mujahed [15] and Xu et al. [14]. However, Xu et al. [14] reported that waste apricot shells do not comprise nitrogen, and the source of nitrogen in their CQDs was the use of nitric acid in their synthesis. Meanwhile, the difference in composition reported by Turna et al. [18] is owed to their Se-based synthesis of the CQDs nanocomposite.

In brief, after reviewing CQDs synthesized from apricots as a green natural resource from different countries and for variant applications, the proposed study showed superiority. Greener energy consumption, faster reaction time and higher QY are plus advantages from green chemistry perspective. The red-shift in fluorescence together with nano-sized stable CQDs structure enhances both the sensitivity and selectivity of the developed zero-dimensional C-skeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing the proposed tool for RAL determination relative to some reported tools
ce:section.ce:para: Several methodologies were reported earlier for determination of RAL using different techniques. The proposed study is the first to be reported for RAL determination using the novel CDs tools. The proposed method was evaluated for RAL determination in comparison to some previously reported techniques that utilized UV-spectrometry [26,47,48], fluorescence spectroscopy [26], atomic absorption spectrometry [47], LC-MS/MS [22,49] or LC-UV [50,51] (SM Table S14).

Some specific limitations could be exhibited for the other reported techniques. Chromatographic techniques for instance, which are widely utilized in laboratories today, present significant drawbacks when compared to spectrofluorimetric tools regarding both cost-effectiveness and sample throughput. The expenses associated with acquiring and maintaining chromatographic systems are considerably higher. Besides, the costs associated with columns, solvents, mobile phases, and other consumables can accumulate significantly, particularly in high sample throughput. In terms of green chemistry, the concept offers a forward-thinking methodology for the formulation and synthesis of chemicals, focusing on mitigating their detrimental effects on the environment. It prioritizes the reduction of organic solvents, reagents, and energy usage in analytical procedures [52]. The extensive use of organic solvents as reported by the other techniques has higher ecological impacts. The innovative N@CQDs probes benefit from the use of water as eco-friendly solvent and doesn't require extraction, which distinguish them from previously documented techniques Moreover, as shown, CQDs shows higher selectivity and enhanced sensitivity as shown from the LOD compared to the reported methods (Supplementary materials Table S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: The characteristics of CQDs synthesized from the same source material can be influenced by several factors, including geographical and botanical organ (leaves, fruits, etc.) origins. Differences in the CQDs chemical composition and structural properties of the source material could be observed, resulting in distinct photoluminescence behaviors and potential applications. Apricots are one important natural resource that has been recently investigated in a few publications for synthesis of CQDs in different geographical regions and for different applications. Reviewing literature, most of this reported research used long synthetic pathways and used apricots in form of biomass waste, grated unripe fruits or apricot shell. In the proposed research, novel CQDs were synthesized from apricots juice was in a single step microwave assisted technique. The properties of the developed CQDs were found improved in terms of quantum yield and photoluminescent characters, when compared to those previously reported from the same natural resource. Moreover, these novel CQDs were utilized for the first time for the determination of ramipril in a turn-off inclusion technique. The method was validated according to guidelines in both aqueous and biological matrices. The unprecedented CQDs tool was applied successfully for determination of ramipril in both tablet dosage forms and human plasma, proving its efficiency for use in routine pharmaceutical quality control and research activities. Finally, this cutting-edge technique yields highly fluorescent green CQDs that's environmentally friendly and naturally resourced that align perfectly with the concepts of green analytical chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Baher I. Salman: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hany A. Batakoushy: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Roshdy E. Saraya: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ahmed I. Hassan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ahmed Al-Harrasi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Adel Ehab Ibrahim: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:para: There are no funds to declare.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
